title: Sarga 53
summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: Themes: action and duty, self-knowledge, mind and meditation. The passage moves from “Having heard that exceedingly wonderful speech of Lakshmana, Rama became very pleased and indeed spoke these words.” through “Indeed, it is heard that formerly there was a very famous king named Nṛga, a protector of the earth, who was devoted to Brahmins, truthful, and…” through “Then that Brahmana called her by her own name, saying, "Come, Shabala!" and the cow indeed heard his voice.”

Themes: self-knowledge, knowledge and discernment. The passage moves from “Having recognized the voice of that hungry Brahmana, the cow indeed followed him from behind as he, radiant like fire, walked away.” through “For many thousands and hundreds of years, you will indeed reside in this pit as a chameleon for a long duration.” through “In this manner, that king experiences that very terrible curse.”

Themes: action and duty. The passage moves from “Indeed, the crowding of petitioners leads to harm for kings; therefore, let the petitioners quickly approach me for an audience.” through “Indeed, the fruit of a well-done work is not lost, O king; therefore, O son of Sumitra, go and wait for any person who has business. इत्यार्षे…”

verses.sanskrit: लक्ष्मणस्य तु तद्वाक्यं निशम्य परमाद्भुतम् । सुप्रीतश्चाभवद्रामो वाक्यमेतदुवाच ह ।। 7.53.1 ।।
verses.translation: Having heard that exceedingly wonderful speech of Lakshmana, Rama became very pleased and indeed spoke these words.

verses.sanskrit: दुर्लभस्त्वीदृशो बन्धुरस्मिन्काले विशेषतः । यादृशस्त्वं महाबुद्धेर्मम सौम्य मनो ऽनुगः ।। 7.53.2 ।।
verses.translation: Indeed, such a kinsman as you, O greatly intelligent and gentle one, who follows my mind, is rare to find, especially in these times.

verses.sanskrit: यच्च मे हृदये किञ्चिद्वर्तते शुभलक्षण । तन्निशामय च श्रुत्वा कुरुष्व वचनं मम ।। 7.53.3 ।।
verses.translation: O one with auspicious marks, listen carefully to what is in my heart, and having heard it, carry out my command.

verses.sanskrit: चत्वारो दिवसाः सौम्य कार्यं पौरजनस्य वै । अकुर्वाणस्य सौमित्रे तन्मे मर्माणि कृन्तति ।। 7.53.4 ।।
verses.translation: O gentle Lakshmana, the neglect of the citizens' work for four days indeed pierces my vital parts.

verses.sanskrit: आहूयन्तां प्रकृतयः पुरोधा मन्त्रिणस्तथा । कार्यार्थिनश्च पुरुषाः स्त्रियश्च पुरुषर्षभ ।। 7.53.5 ।।
verses.translation: O best among men, let the principal officers, the chief priest, the ministers, and also the men and women seeking justice be summoned.

verses.sanskrit: पौरकार्याणि यो राजा न करोति दिने दिने । संवृते नरके घोरे पतितो नात्र संशयः ।। 7.53.6 ।।
verses.translation: There is no doubt that a king who does not attend to the affairs of his citizens daily falls into a terrible, enclosed hell.

verses.sanskrit: श्रूयते हि पुरा राजा नृगो नाम महायशाः । बभूव पृथिवीपालो ब्रह्मण्यः सत्यवाक् शुचिः ।। 7.53.7 ।।
verses.translation: Indeed, it is heard that formerly there was a very famous king named Nṛga, a protector of the earth, who was devoted to Brahmins, truthful, and pure.

verses.sanskrit: स कदाचिद्गवां कोटीः सवत्साः स्वर्णभूषिताः । नृदेवो भूमिदेवेभ्यः पुष्करेषु ददौ नृपः ।। 7.53.8 ।।
verses.translation: That king, Nṛga, once at Pushkara, gave crores of cows, along with their calves and adorned with gold, to the Brahmins.

verses.sanskrit: ततः सङ्गाद्गता धेनुः सवत्सा स्पर्शिता ऽनघ । ब्राह्मणस्याहिताग्नेश्च दरिद्रस्योञ्छवर्तिनः ।। 7.53.9 ।।
verses.translation: O sinless one, then that cow with its calf, having come into contact, was taken by a poor Brahmana who maintained sacred fires and lived by gleaning.

verses.sanskrit: स नष्टां गां क्षुधार्तो वै अन्विषंस्तत्र तत्र च । नापश्यत्सर्वराज्येषु संवत्सरगणान्बहून् ।। 7.53.10 ।।
verses.translation: Afflicted by hunger, he searched for his lost cow here and there in all kingdoms for many years but could not find it.

verses.sanskrit: ततः कनखलं गत्वा जीर्णवत्सां निरामयाम् । ददर्श गां स्वकां धेनुं ब्राह्मणस्य निवेशने ।। 7.53.11 ।।
verses.translation: Then, having gone to Kanakhala, he saw his own cow, whose calf had grown old and was healthy, in the dwelling of the Brahmana.

verses.sanskrit: अथ तां नामधेयेन स्वकेनोवाच स द्विजः । आगच्छ शबलेत्येवं सा तु शुश्राव गौः स्वरम् ।। 7.53.12 ।।
verses.translation: Then that Brahmana called her by her own name, saying, "Come, Shabala!" and the cow indeed heard his voice.

verses.sanskrit: तस्य तं स्वरमाज्ञाय क्षुधार्तस्य द्विजस्य वै । अन्वगात्पृष्ठतः सा गौर्गच्छन्तं पावकोपमम् ।। 7.53.13 ।।
verses.translation: Having recognized the voice of that hungry Brahmana, the cow indeed followed him from behind as he, radiant like fire, walked away.

verses.sanskrit: यो ऽपि पालयते विप्रः सो ऽपि गामन्वगाद्द्रुतम् । गत्वा तमृषिमाचष्ट मम गौरिति सत्वरम् ।। 7.53.14 ।।
verses.translation: The brahmin who was tending the cow also quickly followed it, and having gone, swiftly told that sage, "This is my cow."

verses.sanskrit: स्पर्शिता राजसिंहेन मम दत्ता नृगेण ह । तयोर्ब्राह्मणयोर्वादो महानासीद्विपश्चितोः ।। 7.53.15 ।।
verses.translation: One brahmin claimed the cow was touched by the king, while the other insisted it was given to him by Nṛga, leading to a great dispute between the two learned brahmins.

verses.sanskrit: विवदन्तौ ततो ऽन्योन्यं दातारमभिजग्मतुः । तौ राजभवनद्वारि न प्राप्तौ नृगशासनम् ।। 7.53.16 ।।
verses.translation: Then, disputing with each other, they approached the giver (King Nṛga), but those two did not meet King Nṛga at the palace gate.

verses.sanskrit: अहोरात्राण्यनेकानि वसन्तौ क्रोधमीयतुः । ऊचतुश्च महात्मानौ तावुभौ द्विजसत्तमौ ।। क्रुद्धौ परमसम्प्राप्तौ वाक्यं घोराभिसंहितम् ।। 7.53.17 ।।
verses.translation: After staying for many days and nights, those two great-souled and best among brahmins became exceedingly angry and spoke terrible words filled with a curse.

verses.sanskrit: अर्थिनां कार्यसिद्ध्यर्थं यस्मात्त्वं नैषि दर्शनम् । अदृश्यः सर्वभूतानां कृकलासो भविष्यसि ।। 7.53.18 ।।
verses.translation: Because you do not grant an audience to petitioners for the accomplishment of their purpose, you shall become a chameleon, invisible to all beings.

verses.sanskrit: बहुवर्षसहस्राणि बहुवर्षशतानि च । श्वभ्रे ऽस्मिन् कृकलासो वै दीर्घकालं वसिष्यसि ।। 7.53.19 ।।
verses.translation: For many thousands and hundreds of years, you will indeed reside in this pit as a chameleon for a long duration.

verses.sanskrit: उत्पत्स्यते हि लोके ऽस्मिन्यदूनां कीर्तिवर्धनः । वासुदेव इति ख्यातो विष्णुः पुरुषविग्रहः ।। 7.53.20 ।।
verses.translation: Indeed, in this world, Vishnu, renowned as Vasudeva, the increaser of the Yadus' glory and having a human form, will be born.

verses.sanskrit: स ते मोक्षयिता राजंस्तस्माच्छापाद्भविष्यसि । कृता च तेन कालेन निष्कृतिस्ते भविष्यति ।। 7.53.21 ।।
verses.translation: O King, he will be your liberator from that curse, and by him, your expiation will be accomplished in due course.

verses.sanskrit: भारावतरणार्थं हि नरनारायणावुभौ । उत्पत्स्येते महावीर्यौ कलौ युग उपस्थिते ।। 7.53.22 ।।
verses.translation: Indeed, both Nara and Narayana, greatly powerful, will be born for the purpose of alleviating the earth's burden when the Kali Yuga has arrived.

verses.sanskrit: एवं तौ शापमुत्सृज्य ब्राह्मणौ विगतज्वरौ । तां गां हि दुर्बलां वृद्धां ददतुर्ब्राह्मणाय वै ।। 7.53.23 ।।
verses.translation: Thus, having pronounced the curse, those two Brahmins, now free from distress, indeed gave that weak, old cow to a Brahmin.

verses.sanskrit: एवं स राजा तं शापमुपभुङ्क्ते सुदारुणम् ।। 7.53.24 ।।
verses.translation: In this manner, that king experiences that very terrible curse.

verses.sanskrit: कार्यार्थिनां विमर्दो हि राज्ञां दोषाय कल्पते । तच्छीघ्रं दर्शनं मह्यमभिवर्तन्तु कार्यिणः ।। 7.53.25 ।।
verses.translation: Indeed, the crowding of petitioners leads to harm for kings; therefore, let the petitioners quickly approach me for an audience.

verses.sanskrit: सुकृतस्य हि कार्यस्य फलं नापैति पार्थिवः । तस्माद्गच्छ प्रतीक्षस्व सौमित्रे कार्यवाञ्जनः ।। 7.53.26 ।।
verses.translation: Indeed, the fruit of a well-done work is not lost, O king; therefore, O son of Sumitra, go and wait for any person who has business. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये श्रीमदुत्तरकाण्डे त्रिपञ्चाशः सर्गः ।। 53 ।। Thus ends the fifty-third sarga of Uttara Kanda of the holy Ramayana the first epic composed by sage Valmiki.
